text: The surgeon begins the cholecystectomy by marking the anterior and posterior lines of dissection above the Rouvière's sulcus.
The surgeon makes an incision over the peritoneum, initiating the dissection process in the cholecystectomy procedure.
After dividing the peritoneum, the surgeon uses a Maryland dissector to carefully lower the fibro-fatty tissue from the gallbladder medially and inferiorly towards the common bile duct (CBD).
The surgeon uses a surgical instrument to grasp a small bunch of tissue near the gallbladder, applying a short pulse to manipulate the tissue and push it medially.
The surgeon uses a Maryland dissector to lower the fibro fatty tissue on the medial side of the gallbladder, carefully separating it from surrounding structures.
The surgeon exposes the cystic artery, with a visible lymph node situated over it, alongside the cystic duct.
The surgeon identifies the branch of the cystic artery leading to the cystic duct, visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies a fiber fatty tissue hooked to the gallbladder, preparing to manipulate it for further dissection.
The surgeon applies a brief electrocautery buzz to create a window in the fibro fatty tissue adjacent to the gallbladder, taking care to avoid the biliary tree on the medial side.
The surgeon uses a short pulse of electrocautery to lower the fibro-fatty tissue posteriorly, hugging it to the gallbladder and pushing it inferiorly and medially towards the common bile duct (CBD).
The surgeon visually identifies a prominent artery leading to the cystic duct, with clear anatomical delineation in the surgical field.
The surgeon uses electrocautery to buzz the area around the cystic artery branch to facilitate dissection, aiming to separate the fibro fatty tissue from the gallbladder.
A large stone is visible in the infundibulum of the gallbladder, obstructing the area designated for dissection.
The surgeon milks the stone out of the infundibulum and pushes it into the gallbladder, clearing the area for further dissection.
The surgeon carefully lowers the fibro fatty tissue surrounding the cystic artery, pushing it inferiorly and medially towards the common bile duct.
The surgeon uses electrocautery to buzz the tissue and then applies downward pressure to facilitate further dissection.

The surgeon uses electrocautery to buzz and push the tissue down, creating a window through the fibrous tissue. The gallbladder is then grasped, buzzed again, and pushed further to facilitate dissection.
The surgeon attempts to use electrocautery on the tissue, but the device does not activate properly, leaving the area unaltered.
Bleeding is observed in the surgical field, requiring attention to manage the situation.
The surgeon identifies a small branch of the cystic artery, surrounded by fibrous tissue, and prepares to lower it.
The surgeon uses electrocautery to buzz the tissue effectively before pulling, ensuring proper separation and minimizing bleeding.
The surgeon encounters bleeding after pulling a small branch, as the electrocautery device did not properly coagulate the tissue.
The surgeon applies manual compression and uses bipolar electrocautery to control bleeding above the Rouvière's sulcus, ensuring hemostasis in the area.
The surgeon continues dissection around the cystic duct, carefully skeletonizing it by removing surrounding fibro-fatty tissue and fibers to facilitate secure clipping.
The surgeon prepares to place a clip around the cystic duct, ensuring it is fully skeletonized from surrounding fibro-fatty tissue for secure clipping.

The surgeon carefully delineates fibers around the cystic duct, identifying potential small blood vessels. Using electrocautery, the surgeon buzzes the area without applying traction, ensuring the convexity is kept away from medial vital structures.

The surgeon has completed the dissection of the lower third of the gallbladder, revealing two tubular structures leading towards it. The critical view of safety is achieved, and the cystic artery is clipped, followed by the cystic duct.
The surgeon releases pressure from the traction instrument at the level of the infundibulum to prevent tenting of the critical view of safety (CVT).
The surgeon places two clips on the medial side of the cystic duct and one clip on the specimen side to prevent spillage during the cholecystectomy procedure.
The surgeon begins to detach the gallbladder from the gallbladder bed, carefully separating it from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon proceeds gently to address any minor bleeding points visible in the surgical field, ensuring careful management of oozing blood during the cholecystectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses cautery near the gallbladder, resulting in a small rent in the gallbladder wall.
The surgeon decompresses the gallbladder, reducing its size, and continues with the cholecystectomy procedure.
The gallbladder is visually detached from the liver bed, indicating completion of its separation from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon removes the gallbladder from the surgical field and performs a thorough lavage to cleanse the area.
The surgeon retrieves the gallbladder from the epigastric port following its detachment from the liver bed.
The surgeon prepares the gallbladder bed for hemostasis, ensuring that any bleeding is controlled and the area is stabilized.
The surgeon places a small French drain into the subhepatic space to manage spillage from the procedure.
